advaita.rendering: With a mind disciplined by abhyāsa-yoga (repetition-practice) — that is, by the uninterrupted return of a single mode of awareness to the one object, Brahman, unbroken by any heterogeneous perception — and that mind thus unable to stray toward any other object, the yogin who meditates following the instruction of scripture and teacher reaches the Supreme Person, the Radiant One dwelling in the solar orb. Śaṅkara emphasizes that 'abhyāsa' here is technical: same-genus impressions recurring without alien interruption, not mere mechanical repetition. The 'divine Person in the sun' is cited from śruti; the destination is not a personal deity but the niratiśaya (unsurpassable) substrate that solar imagery points toward.
advaita.key_divergence: Destination is the substratum Brahman; solar-Person imagery is Upaniṣadic pointer, not a Personal God.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: tul'yapratyayāvṛtti-lakṣaṇaḥ... nānyagāmi... paramam niratiśayam puruṣam divye sūryamaṇḍale bhavam

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Day after day — abhyāsa being the constant mental rehearsal of the Beloved in all contexts, yoga being the formal upāsanā (meditative worship) performed at the appointed hours — with a mind that does not wander, meditating on Me, the Supreme Person, in the manner about to be described, the sādhaka reaches Me and becomes conformed to Me in form and glory, just as Bharata became a deer through the force of his final meditation. Rāmānuja explicitly distinguishes abhyāsa (continuous informal rehearsal) from yoga (formal scheduled upāsanā), and the fruit is not absorption but sārūpya (conformity of form) within a qualified non-dual relationship.
viśiṣṭādvaita.key_divergence: Fruit is sārūpya with a personal Bhagavān, not nirguṇa absorption; Bharata-deer analogy warns of unintended consequences and confirms the power of habitual contemplation.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: aharaho abhyāsayogābhyāṃ yuktatayā... madisamānākāro bhavati... abhyāso nityanaimitikāviruddheṣu sarvakāleṣu manasā upāsyasaṃśīlanam

dvaita.rendering: Abhyāsa itself is yoga — there is no separation between the practice and the discipline; constant repetition of awareness directed toward Hari is the entire path. The 'divine Person' (divyam puruṣam) is glossed from Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.5.18: He who dwells in all bodies (puriśaya), fills them completely, from whom nothing is hidden and nothing concealed — that fully present, fully sovereign Hari. 'Divine' (divyam) derives from div + krīḍā: He who sports in creation; the destination is an eternally distinct, fully real, playfully sovereign Lord, never a substrate to be merged into.
dvaita.key_divergence: Abhyāsa and yoga are identical (not two distinct phases as in Rāmānuja); the destination is the fully personal, eternally distinct Hari proven by śruti citation; merger is impossible.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: abhyāsa eva yogo'bhyāsayogaḥ... puriśayam pūrṇam... nainena kiñcanānāvṛtam... divu krīḍā iti dhātoḥ

śuddhādvaita.rendering: After answering all seven questions, Kṛṣṇa now reveals the fruit for yogins, jñānins, and bhaktas at the moment of departure: through abhyāsa-yoga, with a mind that does not move toward any object or even toward the Akṣara, meditating on the Supreme Person Nārāyaṇa, the solar-manifest Divinity, the sādhaka reaches Him. That Person is then described as the Poet-Seer who is more subtle than the atom — subtler even than the jīva — self-luminous as the sun, beyond darkness and Prakṛti; the Puruṣa of the Puruṣasūkta. Vallabha weaves together the BG verse, the Puruṣasūkta, and the phrase 'aṇvīṃ jīvakalāṃ dhyāyet' to insist the destination is Kṛṣṇa as cosmogonic Presence, not an abstract Absolute.
śuddhādvaita.key_divergence: Nān'ya-gāminā glossed as 'not moving even toward the Akṣara' — the destination is beyond even the Imperishable; fruit covers all three paths (yoga, jñāna, bhakti) simultaneously.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: nānyagāminā viṣayādyagāminā(akṣarādyagāminā)... paramam puruṣam nārāyaṇam... puruṣasūktapratipādyasvarūpam

bhakti.rendering: Śrīdhara opens by contextualizing: this verse demonstrates that abhyāsa is the inner (antaraṅga) support of continuous remembrance, established in the preceding verse. Abhyāsa is technically the unbroken flow of homogeneous cognitions (sajātīya-pratyaya-pravāha); that flow itself constitutes the yoga, the means. With a mind made one-pointed by this means — and therefore unable to go to any other object — meditating on the divine, luminous (dyotana-ātmaka) Supreme Lord, the sādhaka reaches Him. The reading is balanced: devotional frame, precise philosophical gloss, no polemical reduction.
bhakti.key_divergence: Explicitly ties this verse to the prior verse's 'continuous remembrance' as its technique; 'divine' read as 'self-luminous by its own nature' (dyotana-ātmaka); no extended cosmology.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: sajātīyapratyayapravāhaḥ sa eva yoga upāyas tena yuktena ekāgreṇa... dyotanātmakaṃ paramaṃ puruṣam... tameva yāti

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Having answered all seven questions, Kṛṣṇa now elaborates the fruit of remembrance at departure. Abhyāsa is the unbroken flow of same-genus cognitions focused on Me, without interruption by heterogeneous cognitions — exactly as defined in Chapter Six — and that same practice is samādhi, yoga. With a mind absorbed in that samādhi, configured entirely to the ātmā-form and empty of all other modes even without deliberate suppression, meditating according to scripture and teacher, the sādhaka reaches the Supreme Person, the solar-dwelling Brahman ('yaś cāsāv āditye' per śruti). Madhusūdana explicitly merges Śaṅkara's technical definition of abhyāsa with the personal devotional telos: the destination is both niratiśaya Brahman and the Bhagavān dwelling in the sun.
advaita-bhakti.key_divergence: Cites Chapter Six's prior definition of abhyāsa as canonical; adds that the mind's one-pointedness in samādhi means wandering ceases naturally (not by forced suppression) — a subtler psychological claim than Śaṅkara; solar śruti citation shared with Advaita but embedded in a devotional frame.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: ātmākāravṛttītaravṛttiśūnyaṃ... nānyagāminā nirodhaprayatnaṃ vināpi... yacca āsāv āditye iti śruteḥ

so_what_questions: If the destination depends on the final moment of consciousness at death (as BG 8.6 insists), what happens to years of practice when a deathbed distraction arises — is the entire sādhana negated, or is habit structurally protective?
Rāmānuja distinguishes abhyāsa (informal daily rehearsal) from yoga (formal timed upāsanā): which of these do contemporary practitioners actually sustain, and does the distinction matter for outcome?

Madhvācārya glosses 'divine' from a Bṛhadāraṇyaka passage about the omnipresent Puruṣa; Śaṅkara glosses the same word from a different śruti about the solar Person — how does the choice of śruti citation control the doctrinal conclusion without changing the Sanskrit text?

Vallabha reads nān'ya-gāminā as 'not moving even toward the Akṣara' — what psychological practice does that require beyond what ordinary non-distraction requires, and what does it imply about intermediate states of liberation?

All six schools agree that the mind must be 'not going elsewhere' — but they disagree on whether this is achieved by suppression, habituation, love, or samādhi's natural exhaustion of alternatives. Which mechanism is falsifiable?

Madhusūdana claims the samādhi-mind stops wandering 'even without deliberate suppression' (nirodhaprayatnaṃ vinā) — is this a higher stage than Chapter Six's abhyāsa that does require effort, or the same stage described from inside rather than outside?

The verse is addressed to Arjuna as 'Pārtha' — son of Pṛthā/Kuntī, not as Arjuna the warrior. Four of six commentators note this address; what is the significance of the maternal-lineage invocation at this point in the instruction?

advaita.application: Morning sitting: choose one object of inquiry — 'Who is aware right now?' — and return to it every time another thought arises, not by force but by recognition. The return itself, repeated without gap for whatever duration is available, is abhyāsa. Do not evaluate the quality of the sitting; evaluate only whether you returned. Over weeks, the mind that once required effort to return begins returning on its own — this is Śaṅkara's 'uninterrupted same-genus cognition' stabilizing as a disposition rather than a practice.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Maintain two registers simultaneously: informal rehearsal (abhyāsa) — brief internal acknowledgments of Bhagavān throughout ordinary activity, at transitions, before meals, at the start of each task — and formal scheduled upāsanā (yoga) at a fixed time each day, however short. Rāmānuja's point is that the formal session deepens the informal thread, and the informal thread keeps the formal session from becoming ritual-only. Missing one formal session is not failure; losing the informal thread is the actual loss.

dvaita.application: Treat every repeated action as an address to a distinct, living, fully present Hari who is not diminished by the repetition. The Madhva reading insists that Hari is not a concept to be internalized but a Person who is genuinely 'other' — sovereign, playful, real. In daily terms: when you return to a name, a form, a mantra in meditation, do so as you would return to a real conversation, not a self-generated internal state. The ontological gap is the practice; the practice is not aimed at closing it.

śuddhādvaita.application: The Vallabha reading demands that nothing — not even a noble spiritual object like the Imperishable — be allowed to become a stopping-point. In practice: when a meditation becomes comfortable, when a framework feels complete, when a spiritual identity feels settled — that is precisely the moment to move. Apply this at work: do not let a successful method become a fixed identity. Kṛṣṇa as the cosmogonic 'more subtle than the subtlest' means the target is always one register more refined than the last stable state.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's 'inner support' framing suggests a layered architecture: external practice (ritual, recitation, schedule) provides the structure, but the actual mechanism is the unbroken interior flow of same-genus awareness. Practically: if you find your outer practice mechanical, the prescription is not more outer practice but investigation of whether the interior flow has broken. Re-establish the interior thread first; the outer practice will re-animate. This applies directly to any sustained skill-building — the inner engagement (sajātīya-pratyaya-pravāha) is the load-bearing element.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's key practical contribution is 'nirodhaprayatnaṃ vinā' — the wandering stops without a suppression effort when the samādhi-quality matures. This is actionable feedback for any meditator: if you are still fighting distractions in meditation after years, the issue is not insufficient effort but insufficient samādhi-ripeness. The prescription is not more suppression but deeper immersion in the object itself — devotion as the accelerant of absorption, not as an alternative to it. Bhakti and jñāna converge in the same attentional mechanism.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present and clean; renderings anchored to distinct Sanskrit glosses; real doctrinal divergence preserved across abhyāsa-definition, destination-ontology, and suppression-vs-absorption mechanism; no HTML artifacts in any payload.
